data.title: TMC the metals company Price Target Maintained With a $4.00/Share by Wedbush
Grains Under Pressure on Tariff FearsMarket Talk
data.description: Tariff concerns are hitting grains, with most-active CBOT corn down 1.5%, soybeans off 0.6%, and wheat sinking 2.3%. Worries about how reciprocal tariffs expected on April 2 will affect commodities demand is weighing on the agricultural complex. "The uncertainty of it all has fear as the dominant factor in the markets heading into the weekend," says Arlan Suderman of StoneX in a note. In particular, Suderman is watching for retaliatory tariffs from Mexico, which would have a big effect on U.S. grain exports. "Retaliatory tariffs by Mexico on Ag commodities could crush demand for vital commodities," he says. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Four policemen, two militants killed in gun battle in India's Kashmir
data.description:
At least four police personnel and two militants were killed in a gun battle in India's restive territory of Jammu and Kashmir, an official said on Friday, as troops searched for heavily armed men who local residents had spotted in the area on Sunday.

Militants have for decades fought security forces in the region, known for its scenic lakes, lush meadows, and snow-topped mountains, resulting in the death of thousands of people, though violence has tapered off in recent years.

Government data show 14 security personnel were killed in such violence in the first half of 2024, and 30 died in 2023.

Kashmir has also been at the heart of over 70 years of hostility between India and Pakistan, both of which claim it in full but rule it in part, and have fought two of their three wars over it.

The encounter this week took place in a forested part of the Kathua area, near India's border with Pakistan, where the men were spotted last week.

"We have recovered bodies of three police men and have also spotted the body of another cop and two militants lying in the forest," the official, who did not want to be named because he was not authorised to speak to the media, told Reuters.

Arms and ammunition, including two grenades, a bulletproof jacket, empty shells and some magazines of assault rifles were also recovered from the area, the official said.

Police and the army are yet to reveal the number of those killed in the operation, but the Governor of Jammu and Kashmir, Manoj Sinha, said in a post on X that "several terrorists" had been "neutralised" in the encounter.

"I bow to the valorous martyrs of J&K (Jammu and Kashmir) Police, who laid down their lives for our motherland...Operation in progress," he added.

Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi's government had, in 2019, revoked the special status granted to Jammu and Kashmir - India's only Muslim-majority region - and split it into two federally administered territories.

"No power in the world" could restore the region's special status, Modi said in November.

data.title: Nvidia-backed CoreWeave's shares likely to open up to 25% above IPO price
data.description:
CoreWeave's (CRWV.O) shares were set to open up to 25% above their offer price in the company's debut on the Nasdaq on Friday.

The Nvidia-backed AI infrastructure firm's stock was indicated to trade at $50, compared with the IPO price of $40.

At the higher end, it could notch a valuation of around $29 billion on a fully diluted basis.

The first major AI startup to list in recent years suffered a setback on Thursday when it downsized its initial public offering.

A strong debut would be a welcome sign, offering hope to other IPO candidates that smooth listings are achievable with tempered valuations, but a lackluster performance may deepen skepticism at a time when equity markets are already grappling with tariff-related turmoil.

"The U.S. IPO market is at an inflection point. This next batch of deals will determine whether the U.S. IPO momentum continues through the second quarter, or whether issuers decide the risk now isn't worth it," said Samuel Kerr, head of equity capital markets at Mergermarket.

The debut will also test the limits of the AI hype, given increasing frustration over Big Tech's massive spending spree and fears of competition from China's artificial intelligence startup DeepSeek.

While investors have propelled AI-related companies such as Nvidia and Microsoft to stratospheric valuations, CoreWeave has stirred concerns among risk-averse investors.

The company provides access to data centers and high-powered Nvidia chips, which have become the most sought-after resource in the race to develop AI applications.

However, 77% of CoreWeave's revenue last year came from just its top two customers, including Microsoft.

At its roadshow, some expressed worries about CoreWeave's heavy reliance on Microsoft as the tech behemoth's shifting AI data center strategy could impact long-term demand for chips.

CoreWeave's capital-intensive business model also raised questions about sustainability, sources said.

"I don't know how receptive the market's going to be," said Kamran Ansari, managing partner at Kapital Ventures, noting that while the growth of the company has been meteoric, its long-term sustainability is yet to be tested.

CoreWeave had around $8 billion in debt as of last year. The company said earlier this month it plans to use about $1 billion of the IPO proceeds to pay down debt.

It also leases its 32 data centers and some equipment instead of owning them, resulting in operating lease liabilities of $2.6 billion.

While investors appear comfortable with the company's high leverage since it has strong free cash flow, the risk of commitments not being fulfilled remains a worry.

The startup has also consistently posted losses, and IPO investors in the last few years have been wary of backing companies with no history of profitability.

FROM CRYPTO TO AI

Founded as an Ethereum-focused crypto miner in 2017, CoreWeave pivoted to AI a few years later. It shuttered its mining business after Ethereum's 2022 upgrade, "The Merge," slashed rewards for miners.

It signed a five-year contract worth $11.9 billion with OpenAI in the lead-up to the IPO, forging ties with the most prominent startup in the industry.

CoreWeave's revenue has also grown at a breakneck pace, climbing more than eight-fold last year.

But it may need to do more to convince investors.

"For most venture-backed deep tech startups, it's going to take more than hype to make it through the public markets right now," said Anthony Georgiades, founder and general partner at Innovating Capital.

CoreWeave's shares are set to trade under the symbol "CRWV". The IPO was underwritten by a syndicate of 18 banks, including Morgan Stanley, J.P.Morgan and Goldman Sachs.

data.title: Argan Shares Jump After Fiscal Q4 Earnings, Revenue Beats
data.description:
Argan shares jumped 25% in recent Friday trading, a day after the company reported higher-than-expected fiscal Q4 net income and revenue.

Diluted earnings per share in the three months ended Jan. 31 rose to $2.22 from $0.89 a year earlier, the company said Thursday.

A lone analyst in a FactSet poll expected $1.20.

Revenue climbed to $232.5 million from $164.6 million a year earlier.

Two analysts surveyed by FactSet expected $197.5 million.

Lake Street upgraded Argan's stock to buy from hold with a price target of $150.

data.title: Two dead as Nepal police use tear gas, sticks to break up pro-monarchy rally
data.description:
Key points:
  • Two killed during protest rally, including a journalist
  • Police say 17 injured, property damaged during rally
  • Protesters are demanding restoration of monarchy
  • Nepal abolished its monarchy nearly 17 years ago

Nepali riot police lobbed tear gas, fired water cannon and used rattan sticks on Friday to break up a protest rally demanding the restoration of constitutional monarchy, and at least two people were killed in the violence, police said.

Authorities said they had to use force to stop thousands of protesters breaking into an area where demonstrations and protest rallies are banned, and they later imposed a curfew in the affected area to stem further escalation of the violence.

The two people killed included one of the protesters and a journalist who was covering the rally, a police spokesman, Dinesh Kumar Acharya, told Reuters. Avenues TV said one of its journalists had died when a house he was in was set ablaze.

Another Nepal police spokesman, Shekhar Khanal, said protesters had set fire to a private house and a vehicle, adding that 17 people including three police personnel were injured. Three protesters are in police custody, he said.

A separate anti-monarchy rally also took place in the Nepali capital on Friday but passed peacefully.

A specially elected assembly scrapped the 239-year-old monarchy in 2008, under an accord that ended a Maoist insurgency which had killed 17,000 people in 1996-2006 and turned Nepal into a secular, federal republic from a Hindu kingdom.

The last king of the Himalayan nation, 77-year-old Gyanendra, has lived with his family in a private house in Kathmandu as a commoner since being toppled.

'UNRULY' CROWD

Friday's trouble erupted when thousands of demonstrators, some carrying Nepal's national flag, hurled stones and tried to break a barricade in order to march towards parliament building in central Kathmandu.

One police official, Kumar Neupane, said police fired in the air to drive away the "unruly" crowd.

A Ministry of Home statement said protesters had vandalised private property, hospital, a political party office, vehicles, a media house and a shopping mall.

Ashok Kumar Bhandari, a spokesman for the Kathmandu district administration, said the curfew declared in the affected area was "for a short period of time, till 10 pm (1615 GMT) but can be extended depending on what turn the situation takes".

Nepal, one of the world's poorest countries, has seen the formation of 14 governments in the 16 years since the abolition of the monarchy. The political instability has stymied economic growth, prompting millions of young people to seek work abroad, mainly in the oil-rich Middle East, South Korea and Malaysia.

Public frustration has been rising over the failure of successive governments to deliver on commitments to develop the economy, which remains reliant on aid and tourism. Nepal is home to eight of the world's 14 highest peaks, including Mount Everest.

data.title: Wall St slips after data fans inflation worries
data.description:
Key points:
  • US consumer spending rises in February
  • US core inflation firmer in February
  • Bullion miners track higher gold prices
  • Lululemon shares slide after bleak annual outlook
  • Indexes off: Dow 0.31%, S&P 500 0.25%, Nasdaq 0.40%

Wall Street's main indexes took a step back on Friday, as fresh data highlighted an uptick in underlying price pressures that surpassed expectations, igniting concerns that the Trump administration's tariff strategies might further fan the flames of inflation.

A Commerce Department report showed the Personal Consumption Expenditures Price index rose in line with what economists polled by Reuters were expecting.

However, excluding volatile items such as food and energy, the index rose more than expected on an annual basis in the previous month, while consumer spending rebounded after falling in January.

Equities have endured pronounced downturns over the past month, fueled by apprehensions that President Donald Trump's policies could usher the economy into an era of elevated inflation and sluggish growth, potentially casting a shadow over the Federal Reserve's monetary policy path.

"This data further lends credence to the marginal shift we're seeing towards a more stagflationary environment," said Jordan Rizzuto, chief investment officer at GammaRoad Capital Partners.

"If inflation is picking up or running hotter coming into a period before we've actually seen the impact from tariffs, that's rather concerning."

Markets continue to anticipate that the central bank will reduce borrowing costs by 25 basis points for the first time this year in July, as per data gathered by LSEG.

Rate-sensitive banking giants such as Citi and Wells Fargo each slipped 0.5%.

Trump's unwavering commitment to a 25% tariff on auto imports, slated to commence next week, has reverberated through global markets, drawing criticism from lawmakers and industry leaders worldwide.

Auto stocks absorbed the impact of the previous session's selloff. General Motors slid 0.7%, while Ford inched down 0.8%.

All eyes are now on a fresh wave of tariffs the U.S. plans to unveil on April 2, with Trump hinting that these measures might deviate from the straightforward tit-for-tat levies previously promised.

In parallel, a report suggested that the European Union is contemplating concessions for Trump following the enactment of reciprocal tariffs.

At 9:41 a.m. ET, the Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 131.65 points, or 0.31%, to 42,168.05, the S&P 500 lost 14.45 points, or 0.25%, to 5,679.04 and the Nasdaq Composite lost 71.38 points, or 0.40%, to 17,732.66.

The unpredictability surrounding tariffs has compelled companies to revise their annual forecasts downward, with Lululemon Athletica being the latest to adjust. Shares of the sportswear maker plunged 11.3%, dragging down the consumer discretionary sector .

Gold miners Harmony Gold and Gold Fields were up over 6% each, tracking higher gold prices.

The S&P 500 is confronting its first quarterly decline in six quarters, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq prepares for its most substantial quarterly drop in nearly two years.

Investors will also parse through speeches by Federal Reserve policymakers Michael Barr and Raphael Bostic later in the day.

U.S. Steel rose 2.8% after a report said Nippon Steel and the company are in active talks about a deal that would preserve their $14 billion merger.

Declining issues outnumbered advancers by a 1.42-to-1 ratio on the NYSE and by a 1.91-to-1 ratio on the Nasdaq.

The S&P 500 posted four new 52-week highs and one new low, while the Nasdaq Composite recorded 22 new highs and 80 new lows.

data.title: January May Mark a Peak for Canadian Economic ActivityMarket Talk
data.description: The latest Canadian GDP numbers likely won't mean much for the Bank of Canada's next interest rate decision, reckons Oxford Economics' Michael Davenport, who continues to expect the central bank will hold steady as it tries to balance the negative hit to demand from tariffs against upside risks to inflation. The economist says the economy got an artificial boost in January as firms pulled forward exports to the U.S. ahead of tariffs, and temporary fiscal stimulus measures continued to support demand. "January may represent the peak in economic activity as the U.S.-Canada trade war pushes Canada's economy into a recession beginning in 2Q," he says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

data.title: Global Energy Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1010 ET - The latest Canadian GDP numbers likely won't mean much for the Bank of Canada's next interest rate decision, reckons Oxford Economics' Michael Davenport, who continues to expect the central bank will hold steady as it tries to balance the negative hit to demand from tariffs against upside risks to inflation. The economist says the economy got an artificial boost in January as firms pulled forward exports to the U.S. ahead of tariffs, and temporary fiscal stimulus measures continued to support demand. "January may represent the peak in economic activity as the U.S.-Canada trade war pushes Canada's economy into a recession beginning in 2Q," he says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1008 ET - Solid Canadian GDP growth in January isn't a sign of things to come. The economy expanded a stronger-than-expected 0.4% on-month, and growth for December was revised up a tick. That, says Capital Economics' Thomas Ryan, shows the economy still had solid momentum to start the year. And the weak preliminary February estimate, showing GDP was flat, can't be blamed entirely o U.S. tariff threats since the winter weather was unseasonably harsh, he adds. Still, Ryan says it adds to the sense the economy is losing momentum, and that's before the full impact of the emerging trade war with the U.S. starts to show up in hard data and likely pushes Canada into a mild recession mid-year. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0957 ET - U.S. natural gas futures are lower as the May contract takes the front month position following a bigger-than-expected storage build that narrowed inventory deficits and confirmed an early start to the injection season. "Four of the past five EIA reports were bearish--suggesting a market struggling to pinpoint supply/demand balances--as storage deficits are evaporating rapidly," Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics says in a note. "The fundamental outlook into next winter is bullish, but soft spring gas prices may be the most-likely scenario first." The front month is off 0.5% at $3.905/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0941 ET - Oil futures are lower but on track for a third straight weekly gain, with WTI hovering below the key $70 level. "Crude futures are still struggling to turn bullish," Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. The coming seasonal ramp up in refineries to meet travel demand, and U.S. sanctionson Venezuelan and Iranian crude are supporting factors, but "the U.S. stock market is struggling, and longer-term demand fears are in the minds of most traders as tariffs begin to kick in on cars not manufactured in the U.S.," he says. WTI is off 0.2% at $69.77 a barrel, and Brent is 0.2% lower at $73.87 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0931 ET - Germany's labor market cooled further in March, and the situation could get worse, Deutsche Bank Research economist Marc Schattenberg says in a note. Seasonally adjusted unemployment ticked up to 6.3% from 6.2% in February, while the number of unemployed rose surprisingly sharply by around 26,000 to 2.92 million, he says. Job losses could persist, especially in the manufacturing sector, not least from the repercussions of U.S. trade policy on key German industries, Schattenberg says. Early labor market indicators don't point to any improvement in the coming months, he says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0929 ET - Germany's labor market continued its gradual weakening in March as recruitment plans continue to shrink, ING's Carsten Brzeski says in a note. Seasonally adjusted unemployment rose by 26,000, increasing the unemployment rate to 6.3% from 6.2% in February. The unadjusted unemployment number of 2.967 million--albeit a fall from February--is the lowest March figure since the financial crisis, he says. Continued announcements of potential cost-cutting measures in the automotive and other industries are set to push up unemployment further, he says. The latest GfK consumer-confidence reading also published Friday suggests German consumers are focusing more on the cooling labor market than on politics even after elections brought added certainty, he says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0911 ET - Canada's economy looks to have flatlined last month after stronger-than-expected growth to start the year, a possible early sign tariff worries are weighing. Statistics Canada forecasts industry-level GDP was little changed in February following growth of 0.4% on-month in January, the strongest expansion since last April. Economists were expecting growth of 0.3% for January, in line with the data agency's earlier flash estimate. January's growth was buoyed largely by both goods-producing industries, with a further rise in mining and oil and gas extraction and a rebound in manufacturing. The biggest drag on growth came from a pullback in retail trade, which the early data shows continued into February. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0521 ET - Euro investment-grade credit spreads have tightened to nearly their tightest historical levels, boosting excess returns on the assets in the year to date, CreditSights analysts say in a note. On aggregate, the excess return gains on euro investment-grade bonds are plus 73 basis points year-to-date, comprised of 51 basis points from spread tightening and 22 basis points from carry, the analysts say. Some of the notable outperforming sectors are utilities, bank surbordinate bonds, insurance subordinate bonds, transportation and autos, CreditSights says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0511 ET - European natural-gas prices are on track for a weekly loss as the heating season wraps up, yet the market heads into the restocking period with notably low storage levels. The benchmark Dutch TTF contract falls 1% to 40.80 euros a megawatt hour in early trade, and is down more than 4% on the week as talks over a cease-fire between Russia and Ukraine pushed prices lower. According to industry group Gas Infrastructure Europe, EU caverns are currently 33.6% full. "Storage levels are likely to fall below 30% by April, significantly below historical levels. This makes it almost impossible for Europe to meet its mandated storage target of 90% by Nov. 1," analysts at ANZ Research say. "With little prospect of a return of Russian pipeline gas, EU member states are increasingly reliant on the LNG market." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0451 ET - Oil prices slide in early trade but are headed for a third consecutive weekly gain as markets weigh the impact of global trade tensions against a tightening supply outlook. U.S. President Trump's latest tariffs on auto imports stocked fears of all-out war that could hurt global demand and raise inflation, with investors now waiting to see the scope of reciprocal tariffs next week. However, renewed pressure on Iranian and Venezuelan crude exports--coupled with signs of strong domestic demand in the U.S.--is raising concerns about near-term tightness in the physical market. "Oil prices have been caught in a tug of war between tariff measures, pulling Brent to the downside, and sanctions measures, pushing it to the upside," analysts at Fitch's BMI research unit say. Brent crude and WTI are both down 0.2% to $73.19 and $69.81 a barrel. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0432 ET - SSE isn't expected to undergo a major strategic review under its incoming CEO Martin Pibworth, Jefferies analysts say in a note. Pibworth is well known to investors and has a deep expertise in energy markets and the execution of large capital projects, Jefferies says. "Overall, we see his appointment as a clear positive for SSE," the analysts say. Shares in the U.K. energy group are up 1.5% at 1,570 pence. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

data.description:
RNS Number : 7516C Pennpetro Energy PLC 28 March 2025  

28 March 2025

Pennpetro Energy Plc

Corporate Update

It was the intention to deliver this overview at the AGM on the 17th of February 2025, however, since the meeting was adjourned, a shortened version thereof is provided below, as well as updates received by the Company regarding audit completion and material progress in other areas since my appointment as CEO in January this year.

It is a privilege to become the Chief Executive Officer of Pennpetro Energy PLC. I take seriously the responsibility of stabilising the Company, ensuring new business development, positive cash inflows and ultimately to deliver growth in shareholder value.

I wish to thank the Board of Directors who have appointed me to lead this organisation and would like to briefly introduce myself for those who do not know me. My career has evolved from audit to oil & gas and the financial services industry. I have dealt with major challenges in less than favourable and complex environments in Africa and elsewhere. The Company's position in the American oil and gas market is timely and provides new and interesting opportunities.

The ultimate success of this Company will only become evident after the completion of the audit and the resumption of the Company's shares to trading on the London Stock Exchange. We will then have the opportunity to attract new investment capital and achieve a higher level of shareholder value by means of investing in commercial upstream partnerships that show long-term growth and viability. To this end, we are in continuing dialogue with our auditors and are able to announce that the audit is progressing and should be on track to be completed in Q2.

Notwithstanding the situation that has evolved, the Company envisages a rewarding future for the shareholders and multiple opportunities are under discussion with contingent funding.

In conclusion, we are focused on the completion of the audit, restoring trading in the Company's shares and transforming PPP into a well- funded trading position in the shortest amount of time.

The Board would like to thank shareholders for their support and the Company looks forward to provide updates in due course.

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1011 ET - The Canadian economy's strong start to 2025 should keep the central bank on hold in April, strategists at TD Securities argue. Monthly GDP growth of 0.4% in January was ahead of expectations and, even with the flash estimate for growth to stall in February, leaves GDP for 1Q tracking near 2% annualized which matches the Bank of Canada's now dated projection from January, they say. The strategists note that growth will come under more pressure after tariffs take effect in March, but the latest data does leave the Canadian economy on a much stronger footing ahead of the trade shock. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1010 ET - The latest Canadian GDP numbers likely won't mean much for the Bank of Canada's next interest rate decision, reckons Oxford Economics' Michael Davenport, who continues to expect the central bank will hold steady as it tries to balance the negative hit to demand from tariffs against upside risks to inflation. The economist says the economy got an artificial boost in January as firms pulled forward exports to the U.S. ahead of tariffs, and temporary fiscal stimulus measures continued to support demand. "January may represent the peak in economic activity as the U.S.-Canada trade war pushes Canada's economy into a recession beginning in 2Q," he says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1008 ET - Solid Canadian GDP growth in January isn't a sign of things to come. The economy expanded a stronger-than-expected 0.4% on-month, and growth for December was revised up a tick. That, says Capital Economics' Thomas Ryan, shows the economy still had solid momentum to start the year. And the weak preliminary February estimate, showing GDP was flat, can't be blamed entirely o U.S. tariff threats since the winter weather was unseasonably harsh, he adds. Still, Ryan says it adds to the sense the economy is losing momentum, and that's before the full impact of the emerging trade war with the U.S. starts to show up in hard data and likely pushes Canada into a mild recession mid-year. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1005 ET - Economists are largely treating Canada's strong GDP increase in January with a shrug. The data, they say, is old news, given President Trump's tariffs to date on Canada aluminum and steel, and more to come next week, highlighted by a 25% levy on automobiles and auto parts. BMO Capital Markets chief economist Doug Porter says January's 0.4% month-over-month increase, and a flattening in February, puts 1Q GDP on the path toward 1.5%-to-2% annualized advance, or largely aligned with Bank of Canada expectations. "The rollicking start to the year is but cold comfort with tariffs now in place and poised to ramp up." Porter says the data show Canada's economy was solid ahead of the escalation in the trade conflict, and should reinforce BOC's "newfound lean to the sidelines" in April. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1005 ET - U.S. credit spreads could widen further, beyond the recent widening recorded last month, as uncertainty around the U.S. economic outlook grows, BlackRock's Amanda Lynam and Dominique Bly say in a note. "This rebuilding of risk premia should reflect a more challenging growth-inflation mix in the U.S., as well as heightened policy uncertainty," they say. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0946 ET - Gold prices soar to a fresh record high in afternoon trade, reaching a peak of $3,124.40 a troy ounce as concerns over global trade tensions and an uncertain economic outlook spur safe-haven demand. Futures now trade 0.7% higher at $3,111.80 a troy ounce and are up nearly 3% on the week. "The gold rally is not quite over yet," says Barbara Lambrecht, commodity analyst at Commerzbank Research. "We consider gold to be well supported for the time being, especially as next week's potentially disappointing U.S. economic indicators could strengthen hopes of more interest rate cuts by the Fed." Meanwhile, the latest U.S. data showed the PCE price index--the Federal Reserve's preferred gauge of inflation--rose 2.5% in the 12 months through February, still above the central bank's 2% target. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

February's 12-month core PCE reading was 2.8%, up from January's upwardly revised 2.7% and from a WSJ consensus forecast of 2.7%. "Treasury Yields Fall as Inflation Remains Above Target — Market Talk," published at 8:48 a.m. ET incorrectly said January's PCE reading was 2.6%.

0938 ET - Euro investment-grade bonds remain attractive relative to their sovereign bond peers, LBBW credit analysts Michael Koehler and Benedikt Horwedel say in a note. "The yield advantage of euro corporate bonds versus German government bonds have been clearly reflected in total returns in recent years." Euro investment-grade bonds are likely to continue performing better than their sovereign counterparts, the analysts say. Euro investment-grade bonds offer higher yields than equivalent government bonds and better stability than their junk bond peers, the analysts say. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0931 ET - Germany's labor market cooled further in March, and the situation could get worse, Deutsche Bank Research economist Marc Schattenberg says in a note. Seasonally adjusted unemployment ticked up to 6.3% from 6.2% in February, while the number of unemployed rose surprisingly sharply by around 26,000 to 2.92 million, he says. Job losses could persist, especially in the manufacturing sector, not least from the repercussions of U.S. trade policy on key German industries, Schattenberg says. Early labor market indicators don't point to any improvement in the coming months, he says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0929 ET - Germany's labor market continued its gradual weakening in March as recruitment plans continue to shrink, ING's Carsten Brzeski says in a note. Seasonally adjusted unemployment rose by 26,000, increasing the unemployment rate to 6.3% from 6.2% in February. The unadjusted unemployment number of 2.967 million--albeit a fall from February--is the lowest March figure since the financial crisis, he says. Continued announcements of potential cost-cutting measures in the automotive and other industries are set to push up unemployment further, he says. The latest GfK consumer-confidence reading also published Friday suggests German consumers are focusing more on the cooling labor market than on politics even after elections brought added certainty, he says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0911 ET - Canada's economy looks to have flatlined last month after stronger-than-expected growth to start the year, a possible early sign tariff worries are weighing. Statistics Canada forecasts industry-level GDP was little changed in February following growth of 0.4% on-month in January, the strongest expansion since last April. Economists were expecting growth of 0.3% for January, in line with the data agency's earlier flash estimate. January's growth was buoyed largely by both goods-producing industries, with a further rise in mining and oil and gas extraction and a rebound in manufacturing. The biggest drag on growth came from a pullback in retail trade, which the early data shows continued into February. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0907 ET - Canadian aluminum and steel producers, in the spotlight after the Trump administration slapped 25% tariffs on imports of the metals, rely heavily on trade between the countries. Exports to the U.S. accounted for 16.8% of Canadian GDP and more than 2.6 million Canadian jobs in 2023, the most recent year where value-added data is available. Statistics Canada notes alumina and aluminum production and processing industry had 68% of output attributed to direct exports to the U.S. Canada's steel industry and iron and steel mills and ferro-alloy manufacturing industry were also highly reliant on exports to the U.S., with 69% and 66% of their output attributed to U.S. demand, respectively. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0856 ET - H&M is struggling to regain momentum, and the challenging macroeconomic environment and unfavorable purchasing conditions are likely to further complicate its recovery, AlphaValue analyst Jie Zhang writes. The company reported weak first quarter results, with slower top-line growth and profitability missing consensus expectations. March sales are expected to rise by just 1%, highlighting continued demand weakness, Zhang says. Profitability remains under pressure from external headwinds, higher discounting, and ongoing investments in customer offerings. In addition, the U.S. remains H&M's second-largest market by revenue, while the group continues to depend heavily on Asian sourcing. "As such, new U.S. tariffs will inevitably impact H&M's trading." Shares fall 0.6%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

The Nvidia-backed AI infrastructure firm's stock was indicated to trade at $50, compared with the IPO price of $40.

At the higher end, it could notch a valuation of around $29 billion on a fully diluted basis.

data.title: Consumer sentiment falls further in March, hits lowest level in late 2022
data.description:
The final reading of consumer sentiment in March fell to 57.0 from a preliminary 57.9 released earlier in the month, the University of Michigan said Friday.

The index is at its lowest level since November 2022.

This is breaking news. Click here for updates.

data.title: Liquidia Says FDA to Review New Drug Application for Yutrepia
data.description:
Liquidia said Friday the US Food and Drug Administration accepted its resubmitted new drug application for Yutrepia inhalation powder to treat pulmonary arterial hypertension and pulmonary hypertension associated with interstitial lung disease.

The company said the FDA classified the resubmission as a complete, Class 1 response to an August 2024 action letter that granted tentative approval of Yutrepia, or treprostinil.

The agency set a May 24 target action date, Liquidia said.

Shares of the company were up 1.5% in recent trading Friday.

data.title: Wolfspeed Shares Fall Sharply After Trading Resumes
data.description:
Wolfspeed shares dropped past 45% in recent Friday trading following the resumption of trading that was paused due to volatility.

On Thursday, the company announced the appointment of Robert Feurle as chief executive officer, effective May 1. Feurle succeeds Thomas Werner, who is serving as interim executive chairman and will return as board chairman after the transition, the company said.
